workflow: Set review_decision to accepted or rejected.
Accepted entries must complete the curation block before fixture build.
Reviewed files can be passed to build_manual_fixture_from_review_candidates.py.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Typical Air Face Velocity for Dehumidifying Coils
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selected in the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
For
information on calculating a dehumidi fying coil’s sensible heat ratio,
see the section on Performance of Dehumidifying Coils, or Appen-dix D of Cooling and Heating Load Calculation Principles  (Peder-
sen et al. 1998). For a given coil surface design and arrangement, therequired sensible heat ratio may be  satisfied by wide variations in
and combinations of air face velocity, in-tube temperature, flow rate,entering air temperature, coil depth, etc., although the variationsmay be self limiting. The maximum coil air face velocity should belimited to a value that prevents water carryover into the air ductwork.Dehumidifying coils for comfort applications are frequently selectedin the range of 2.0 to 2.5 m/s air face velocity.
Operating ratings of dehumidifyi ng coils for factory-assembled,
self-contained air conditioners are generally determined in conjunc-
tion with laboratory testing for the system capacity of the complete
unit assembly.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence directly states typical air face velocity range for dehumidifying coils.

structured_payload_candidate.value: 54 L/s per kW, max 60 L/s per kW
structured_payload_candidate.title: Standard Rating Point for Unitary Equipment
structured_payload_candidate.summary: A standard rating point has been 54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s per kilowatt for unitary equipment.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
For example, a standard rating point has been
54 L/s per kilowatt of refrigeration effect, not to exceed 60 L/s perkilowatt for unitary equipment. Re frigerant (e.g., R-22) duty would
be 3 to 6 K superheat for an appr opriate balance at 7°C saturated
suction. For water coil s, circuitry would operate at 1.2 m/s, 5.5°C
inlet water, 6.7 K rise (or 36 mL /s per kilowatt of refrigeration
effect). The standard ratings at 26.7°C db and 19.5°C wb are repre-
sentative of the entering air conditions encountered in many com-
fort operations.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence explicitly provides standard rating point for unitary equipment.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Freezing Condition for Dehumidification Coils
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C saturated.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
In ho t, humid climates, the light-load
condition has a higher proportion of moisture and a correspond-
ingly lower proportion of sensible  heat. The result is higher dew
points in the conditioned spaces during light-load  conditions un-
less a special means for controlling inside dew points (e.g., reheator dual path) is used.
Fin surface freezing at light lo ads should be avoided. Freezing
occurs when a dehumidification coil’s surface temperature fallsbelow 0°C. Freezing does not occur with standard coils for comfort
installations unless the refrigerant evaporating temperature at the
coil outlet is below –4 to –2°C satu rated; the exact value depends on
the design of the coil, its opera ting dew point, and the amount of
loading. With coil and condensing units to balance at low tempera-
tures at peak loads (not a custom ary design choice), freezing may
occur when load suddenly decreases.
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence describes coil freezing condition with specific saturated temperature range.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Airflow Resistance Reduction with Hydrophilic Coating
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Tests have shown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed to a new untreated coil.

candidate_entries.evidence_text:
Therefore, final selection involves
economic balancing of the initial co st of the coil against the operat-
ing costs of the coil geometry co mbinations available to adequately
meet the performa nce requirements.
The aluminum fin surfaces of new dehumidifying coils tend to in-
hibit condensate sheeting action until they have aged for a year. Hy-
drophilic aluminum fin surface coatings reduce water droplet surface
tension, producing a more evenly dispersed wetted surface action at
initial start-up. Manufacturers have tried different methods of apply-
ing such coatings, including dippi ng the coil into a tank, coating the
fin stock material, or subjecting the material to a chemical etchingprocess. Tests have s hown as much as a 30% reduction in air pressure
drop across a hydrophilic coil as opposed a new untreated coil..
candidate_entries.judge_reason: Evidence mentions up to 30% reduction in air pressure drop due to hydrophilic coating.
